project_overview.project_development_objective: To respond promptly and effectively to an eligible crisis or emergency in Guinea
project_overview.total_financing: Not specified in document (contingent financing amount to be determined upon activation)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Affected citizens of Guinea with focus on vulnerable groups including women, children, elderly, and people with disabilities. Target of 500,000 people at risk (3.77% of total population)

primary_locations: National coverage - all regions of Guinea
Conakry (priority as economy and port hub)
Coastal areas (vulnerable to sea level rise)
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - Guinea is classified as a fragile state. Political transition ongoing with demonstrations banned since May 2022. Risk of political unrest and violence. Spillover risks from Sahel crisis mentioned. Socio-political unrest identified as contingency risk.

key_components.description: Direct emergency income support through scaling cash transfers and cash-for-work programs to affected households. Includes time-bound cash top-ups to existing social cash transfer households and new eligible households identified through social registry/Unified Beneficiary Registry. Target: 300,000 households

key_components.component_name: Provision of Essential Emergency Supplies and Services
key_components.description: Procurement and distribution of critical supplies and services including water, emergency response supplies, medicines, medical equipment, veterinary medicines, food assistance, agricultural inputs, shelter supplies. Target beneficiaries: 225,000 households for food assistance, 75,000 for agricultural inputs, 100,000 for water/sanitation, 120,000 for health services, 1,500 for shelter

Incremental operational expenditures for government response including evacuation, shelter administration, transportation, utilities, staff overtime, equipment rental (generators, debris removal equipment). Technical assistance for recovery, specialized expertise, and preparation of technical documents

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: National Agency for Disasters and Humanitarian Emergencies Management (ANGUCH) in twinned approach with Project Implementation Unit (PIU) of Emergency Response and Nafa Program Support Project (PRU-APN P168777) anchored at National Agency for Social and Economic Inclusion (ANIES)
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Steering Committee (PSC) chaired by Ministry of Economy and Finance (MEF) including representatives from Ministry of Budget, Ministry of Territorial Administration and Decentralization, Ministry of Security and Civil Protection, Ministry of Agriculture and Livestock, Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development, Ministry of Women/Childhood/Vulnerable Persons, Ministry of Health. Standing Emergency Response Committee (SERC) chaired by CERP Project Coordinator, led by ANGUCH. Permanent Inter-Agency Strategic Committee (CoPIA-S) co-chaired by Minister of Territorial Administration and UN Resident Coordinator. CoPIA-Technical Committee includes government, UN agencies, NGOs
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: Fast-track emergency procurement procedures under IPF Policy paragraph 12. Streamlined procurement arrangements justified in Procurement Strategy for Development (PPSD). Framework Agreements with UN agencies (FAO, UNOPS) and private sector logistics providers. Direct contracting, expedited approvals permitted. Systematic Tracking of Exchanges in Procurement (STEP) system used. Positive list of eligible expenditures in CERP Manual
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: PRU-APN responsible for M&E. Annual meetings between PRU-APN, ANGUCH, MEF, World Bank to review portfolio and update CERP Manual. If activated: bi-annual reporting to Steering Committee and World Bank. If not activated: annual readiness reporting. Completion report 6 months after activation period ends. Implementation Completion and Results Report (ICR) if activated at least once. Results framework includes PDO indicators and intermediate indicators by component with bi-annual data collection

identified_risks.risk_description: Political transition with all political demonstrations banned since May 2022. Political unrest and protests may continue. Opposition demonstrations have turned violent with civilian deaths. Uncertainty about election deadline in October 2025. Governance weaknesses including lack of capacity, administrative bottlenecks, delays, corruption risks, and institutional overlaps may hamper CERP implementation
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Flexible and responsive WBG posture with close dialogue with authorities. Possibility of further Country Engagement Note if medium-term programming not possible. Exploration of additional funding eligibility for governance/fragility risks. Use of OP 7.30 if circumstances require. Strict monetary management controls, transparent procurement, regular audits and reporting

identified_risks.risk_description: Elevated downside risks from price/supply disruptions, global economic/climate shocks leading to prolonged inflation, higher financing needs, food insecurity. Bauxite price shocks could reduce exports/revenues. CERP implementation affected if macro-economic/fiscal context unhealthy, diverting resources to other priorities
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: World Bank will monitor macroeconomic pressure through MPO and other instruments. Collaboration with IMF to ensure program alignment with Guinea's macroeconomic needs and limitations

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Twinned approach between PRU-APN PIU and ANGUCH is innovative but untested. Risk if PIU does not dedicate sufficient time to supervise/validate CERP actions or fails to transfer knowledge to ANGUCH. Emergency response complexity with multiple stakeholders having similar/competing responsibilities. Institutional roles and responsibilities remain unclear and overlapping. Lack of unified national leadership. Insufficient human and financial resources. Limited capacity among key actors
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Bank will use experienced PIU (PRU-APN) to support first CERP activation. Prior to activation, PRU-APN will provide training to ANGUCH and designate fiduciary/E&S focal points for close follow-up. ANGUCH will delegate technical functions to agencies with relevant expertise while ensuring cross-sectoral coordination. Capacity assessment to identify positions needed (Procurement, Financial Management, E&S specialists). Tabletop exercises and simulations conducted annually. Rapid after-action reviews following each activation

identified_risks.risk_description: ANGUCH lacks previous World Bank financial management and procurement experience. Weak fiduciary practices and poor expenditure management persist. Guinea's CPIA rating for transparency/accountability/corruption is 2.5 (below 2.7 SSA IDA average). Strengthening needed in FM, accounting controls, disbursement, procurement systems, human resource capacity. Mixed PIU approach is new and untested
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Support from PRU-APN PIU which has sound financial management. Staffing to fill capacity gaps. Fraud and corruption mitigation measures. Increased follow-ups and monitoring of procurement processes. PRU-APN PIU will ensure accurate transaction recording, timely financial statements, asset protection, and Bank-compliant verification arrangements. Quarterly unaudited interim financial reports within 45 days of quarter end. Annual audited financial statements within 6 months of year end

identified_risks.risk_description: Lack of unified national leadership in DRM. Weak Early Warning System (EWS). Process for declaring disaster-related emergency is unclear. 2020 Constitution (suspended) lacks provisions on climate/disaster emergencies. Law 96-9 does not specify conditions/procedures for emergency declarations. National Plan for Disaster Prevention and Relief Organizations (NPDPRO) does not yet exist. Institutional roles overlapping
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Establishment of CENAGCUE and creation of ANGUCH are significant developments. Laws/policies clarifying procedures for declaring disaster-related emergencies need updating. Analytical study to help frame NPDPRO. CERP Manual will detail operational aspects and ensure accountability. Manual updated at least annually and as needed

identified_risks.risk_category: Technical Design of Project or Program
identified_risks.risk_description: First application of RRO through CERP in Guinea requires considerable implementation capacity and coordination for rapid post-emergency response. Emergency response complexity with multiple stakeholders
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Technical Assistance provided in key areas. Use of emergency procedures (direct contracting, expedited approvals, specialized UN agencies). Supply market assessments conducted periodically. Framework Agreements established with suppliers/contractors. Ready to Respond (R2R) diagnostics conducted. Streamlined R2R assessments to identify gaps and enable targeted improvements

identified_risks.risk_description: Multiple institutional stakeholders with overlapping responsibilities. Sectoral agencies lack trained personnel for disaster response, damage assessments, recovery planning. Limited community participation mechanisms
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Citizen engagement encouraged through community-based organizations, Red Cross, volunteers, students. CERP financed activities will encourage use of community/civil society organizations. Grievance Redress Mechanism in place through project-level mechanisms and World Bank's Grievance Redress Service (GRS) and Accountability Mechanism

identified_risks.risk_description: Moderate E&S risk classification. Potential environmental and social impacts from emergency response activities
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) and Environmental and Social Management Plan (ESMP) prepared. Environmental and Social Commitment Plan (ESCP) in place. CERP Manual provides guidance for ESF compliance. Positive list of eligible expenditures designed to minimize E&S risks. Activities within existing footprint. E&S documents disclosed May 14, 2025. Changes to ESCP require Bank agreement formalized through exchange of letters

triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS 1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts (Relevant)
ESS 10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure (Relevant)
ESS 3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management (Relevant)
ESS 6: Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources (Relevant)
ESS 5: Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement (Not Currently Relevant)
ESS 7: Indigenous Peoples/Sub-Saharan African Historically Underserved Traditional Local Communities (Not Currently Relevant)
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Project-level grievance mechanisms established. World Bank's Grievance Redress Service (GRS) available at http://www.worldbank.org/GRS. World Bank's Accountability Mechanism (Inspection Panel and Dispute Resolution Service) available at https://accountability.worldbank.org. Communities and individuals can submit complaints after concerns brought to Bank Management attention
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Gender-informed approach. Women and girls in Guinea face significant barriers. Guinea ranked 182/191 in UN Gender Inequality Index (2021). Women and children 14 times more likely than men to die in disasters. M&E framework includes intermediate indicators: (a) beneficiaries receiving cash transfers/livelihood support of which are female, (b) beneficiaries of emergency food assistance of which are women. Target: 50% female beneficiaries across all components. Specific focus on women's and children's needs in disaster response
vulnerable_groups: Households experiencing frequent shocks (nearly two-thirds reported at least one shock)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project designed to respond to eligible crises including natural disasters and man-made emergencies. Spillover of Sahel crisis identified as contingency risk. Socio-political unrest included in national multi-risk contingency plan. Project activities (cash transfers, essential supplies, coordination) are conflict-sensitive and support vulnerable populations affected by various crises. Community-based organizations and civil society encouraged for response. No direct conflict mitigation activities but emergency response supports affected populations
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Guinea classified as fragile state. Key fragility factors: (1) Political transition with demonstrations banned since May 2022, political unrest and violence risk; (2) Governance weaknesses - lack of capacity, administrative bottlenecks, corruption risks, institutional overlaps; (3) Weak institutional capacity for DRM - unclear roles, overlapping responsibilities, insufficient resources; (4) Limited response capacity - weak Early Warning System, inadequate equipment/logistics; (5) High vulnerability to natural hazards - 22 identified hazards including flooding, epidemics, drought, fires; (6) Climate change amplifying existing fragilities; (7) Food insecurity affecting 17% of population; (8) Frequent household shocks eroding livelihoods; (9) Absence of robust national social protection system; (10) INFORM Risk Index 4.2/10 (medium risk); (11) ND-GAIN Index ranking 154/193 for climate vulnerability; (12) Among countries with steepest rise in disaster risk 2015-2024
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Political security risks: political demonstrations banned since May 2022, opposition demonstrations have turned violent with civilian casualties, uncertainty about election deadline October 2025. Institutional security: weak governance, corruption risks, administrative delays. Operational security: emergency response coordination complexity, multiple stakeholders with overlapping responsibilities. Kaloum oil depot explosion December 2023 demonstrated overstretched response capacity. Mitigation through strict monetary controls, transparent procurement, regular audits, close dialogue with authorities, flexible WBG posture
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: no - Document does not mention displacement or refugee populations as current issue. However, emergency response activities include shelter administration and provision of emergency shelter supplies (target 1,500 households), suggesting preparedness for potential displacement from disasters. Water supply and sanitation support in temporary relocation sites mentioned, indicating readiness for displaced populations
